quotes.text: Go for a business that any idiot can run - because sooner or later, any idiot probably is going to run it.
Know what you own, and know why you own it.
The P/E ratio of any company that's fairly priced will equal its growth rate.
The best stock to buy may be the one you already own.
The amateur investor has advantages over professional investors because they're not constrained by benchmark thinking.
In the long run, a stock's performance is tied to company earnings. Nothing else matters as much.
Behind every stock is a company. Find out what it's doing.
If you can find a company that can grow earnings 20-25% a year, you might have found a ten-bagger over a decade.
Go to five companies in an industry, ask each of them intelligent questions about the other four, and you'll get a good understanding of all five.
The greatest investment reward comes to those who by good luck or good sense find the occasional company that over the years can grow in sales and profits far more than industry as a whole.
A good management will be alert to new products, new processes, new services that can create new profit centers, new sources of growth.
If the job has been correctly done when a common stock is purchased, the time to sell it is almost never.
